title: Built-in Collections
description: Python's standard library collections are your first-choice tools in any FAANG interview. Knowing their internals, exact complexities, and FAANG-grade patterns is what separates L4 from L5.

sections.title: list — Dynamic Array
sections.description:
Python lists are dynamic arrays backed by contiguous memory. O(1) amortized append, O(1) index access, O(N) insert/delete at arbitrary position (shifts elements).

FAANG gotchas: list.pop(0) is O(N). list.insert(0, x) is O(N). Use deque for O(1) front operations.
sections.codeBlock:
# Common list operations and their complexity
arr = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6]

arr.append(7)         # O(1) amortized
arr.pop()             # O(1) — from right
arr.pop(0)            # O(N) — avoid for queues
arr.insert(2, 99)     # O(N) — shifts elements
arr.remove(99)        # O(N) — finds first occurrence
arr.index(5)          # O(N) — linear scan
5 in arr              # O(N) — use set for O(1)

arr.sort()            # O(N log N) — Timsort, in-place
sorted_arr = sorted(arr)  # O(N log N) — returns new list

# List comprehension — preferred over map/filter
squares = [x*x for x in range(10)]           # O(N)
even = [x for x in arr if x % 2 == 0]        # O(N)
matrix = [[0]*3 for _ in range(3)]           # O(N*M) — correct 2D init

# WRONG: [[0]*3]*3 creates 3 references to the SAME row — common bug
bad_matrix = [[0]*3]*3
bad_matrix[0][0] = 99
print(bad_matrix[1][0])  # 99 — bug! all rows share same object

sections.title: dict — Hash Map
sections.description:
Python dicts are hash tables with open addressing. O(1) amortized get/set/delete, O(N) worst case with hash collisions (mitigated by hash randomization). Ordered since Python 3.7 — insertion order is preserved.

FAANG patterns: Frequency maps, memoization caches, index maps, adjacency lists.
sections.codeBlock:
from collections import defaultdict, Counter, OrderedDict

# defaultdict — avoids KeyError, auto-initializes
freq = defaultdict(int)
for ch in 'hello world':
    freq[ch] += 1   # no need to check if key exists

graph = defaultdict(list)
graph['A'].append('B')   # no KeyError if 'A' not seen before

# Counter — frequency map with extras
words = ['apple', 'banana', 'apple', 'cherry', 'banana', 'apple']
c = Counter(words)
print(c.most_common(2))   # [('apple', 3), ('banana', 2)]
print(c['apple'])         # 3
print(c['missing'])       # 0 — never raises KeyError

# Counter arithmetic
a = Counter('aab')
b = Counter('abb')
print(a + b)   # Counter({'a': 3, 'b': 3})
print(a - b)   # Counter({'a': 1}) — keeps only positive counts
print(a & b)   # Counter({'a': 1, 'b': 1}) — min of each
print(a | b)   # Counter({'a': 2, 'b': 2}) — max of each

# LRU Cache using OrderedDict
class LRUCache:
    def __init__(self, capacity):
        self.cache = OrderedDict()
        self.cap = capacity
    def get(self, key):
        if key not in self.cache: return -1
        self.cache.move_to_end(key)  # mark as recently used
        return self.cache[key]
    def put(self, key, value):
        self.cache[key] = value
        self.cache.move_to_end(key)
        if len(self.cache) > self.cap:
            self.cache.popitem(last=False)  # evict LRU (front)

sections.title: set & frozenset — Hash Set
sections.description: Sets provide O(1) average membership test, add, and remove. Backed by the same hash table as dict. Use frozenset for hashable sets (usable as dict keys or in sets of sets).

sections.title: deque — O(1) Both Ends
sections.description: collections.deque (doubly-ended queue) is a doubly linked list of fixed-size blocks. O(1) append and pop from both ends. The correct tool for BFS queues, sliding windows, and any FIFO/LIFO that needs front access.
sections.codeBlock:
from collections import deque

dq = deque([1, 2, 3], maxlen=5)  # maxlen: auto-evict oldest on overflow

# O(1) operations
dq.append(4)       # add to right
dq.appendleft(0)   # add to left
dq.pop()           # remove from right
dq.popleft()       # remove from left
dq.rotate(1)       # rotate right: last element becomes first
dq.rotate(-1)      # rotate left: first element becomes last

# BFS with deque
def bfs_levels(root):
    if not root: return []
    queue = deque([root])
    levels = []
    while queue:
        level_size = len(queue)
        level = []
        for _ in range(level_size):  # process one level
            node = queue.popleft()
            level.append(node.val)
            if node.left: queue.append(node.left)
            if node.right: queue.append(node.right)
        levels.append(level)
    return levels

sections.title: FAANG Patterns — Frequency Map & Sliding Window
sections.description: These patterns combine built-in collections with algorithmic thinking. They appear in 30%+ of FAANG coding questions.

details:
dict vs defaultdict vs Counter: Use dict when all keys are known. Use defaultdict when you need auto-initialization of missing keys. Use Counter when you're counting occurrences — it adds most_common, arithmetic ops, and never raises KeyError for missing keys. Counter(iterable) is O(N) and very idiomatic.

Hashability Contract: Objects used as dict keys or set elements must be hashable — they must implement __hash__ and __eq__, and equal objects must have equal hashes. Mutable built-ins (list, dict, set) are NOT hashable. Use tuple, frozenset, or a custom frozen dataclass instead.

Memory layout: Python list stores pointers (8 bytes each on 64-bit), not the actual objects. A list of 1M ints uses ~8MB just for pointers + the int objects. For numeric data, use array.array or numpy.ndarray to store values directly (no per-object overhead).
